title: Solar Cooker IoT Testing Platform
text:
MSc Research • University of Nairobi
  
  A low-cost, open-source framework for linking standardized performance to the multidimensional impacts of solar cookers - replacing $10,000+ lab equipment with a sub-$100 IoT system.
  🔒 Private Repository

title: Solar Cooker IoT Testing Platform
text:
Problem
Solar cookers can significantly reduce fuel use, indoor air pollution, and CO₂ emissions in developing regions. But validating their performance requires expensive lab-grade instrumentation that most organizations can’t afford.
The ASAE S580.1 standard defines how to measure cooking power, but no affordable, field-ready testing system existed. This creates a data vacuum - innovators and institutions in the Global South cannot generate the verified performance evidence needed for results-based climate financing.
The cost barrier: Reference-grade pyranometers and data loggers cost upwards of $10,000. My target: under $100.

title: Solar Cooker IoT Testing Platform
text:
Solution
I designed a complete testing system spanning hardware, simulation, and software:
Hardware - ESP32-Based Sensor Node

ESP32 microcontroller with MicroPython firmware
BPW34 silicon PIN photodiode with PTFE cosine corrector (irradiance measurement)
MCP6022 dual op-amp transimpedance amplifier (signal conditioning)
PT100 RTD + MAX31865 (water temperature)
DHT22 (ambient temperature/humidity)
Anemometer (wind speed)
SIM808 GSM module (telemetry), MicroSD (local storage)
Custom PCB designed in KiCad - BOM total ~$1.58 for the analog front-end

Simulation & Validation Pipeline (Python)

SciPy - automated analog component optimization (E-series resistor snapping)
PySpice/Ngspice - multi-domain circuit simulation (DC, AC, transient, noise analysis)
9-stage CI/CD hardware pipeline: analytical design → E-series snap → circuit synthesis → SPICE simulation → KiCad PCB export → PCB layout (FreeRouting) → DRC/Gerber export → firmware generation → calibration validation
Feedback loop: re-optimizes analog design if SPICE results diverge from analytical predictions

Software - R Backend + Dashboard

R Plumber REST API for data ingestion, querying, device management, test sessions, and ASAE S580.1 analysis
SQLite database with migration-based schema
Golem-structured Shiny dashboard for real-time visualization
Automatic standardization of cooking power to 700 W/m² per ASAE S580.1
Sustainability impact calculators: fuel savings (kg/year), CO₂ avoided (tonnes), health benefit proxies

title: Solar Cooker IoT Testing Platform
text:
Results

Delivered a field-ready testing system at a fraction of commercial equipment cost - enabling standardized solar cooker evaluation where it was previously impossible.

Design Specifications:

Cost: Sub-$100 (vs. $10,000+ for gold standard)
ADC: ESP32 12-bit, ENOB validated
Bandwidth: ≥ 100 kHz target
Phase margin: ≥ 60 degrees
Noise floor: < 1 ADC LSB (0.8 mV)
Linearity error: < 0.5%
Calibration: ISO 9847 + ASAE S580.1

Impact:

Compliant with the ASAE S580.1 international standard
Testing conducted at UoN’s SCI-accredited Regional Testing and Knowledge Centre
Open-source design allows replication by researchers and NGOs
6 GitHub Actions CI workflows: app, Docker, firmware, hardware, simulations, thesis

title: Solar Cooker IoT Testing Platform
text:
Tech Stack

  ESP32
  MicroPython
  BPW34 Photodiode
  PT100 RTD
  Python
  SciPy
  PySpice / Ngspice
  KiCad
  R Plumber API
  Golem / Shiny
  Docker Compose
  GitHub Actions CI
  Quarto

title: SHA Integrated Health System
text:
Full Stack Engineer • Systems Architect
  
  A health insurance platform designed and architected for Kenya's Social Health Authority (SHA) - targeting 54 million citizens across 47 counties and 13,000+ health facilities.
  🔒 Private Repository

title: SHA Integrated Health System
text:
Problem
When Kenya transitioned from the National Health Insurance Fund (NHIF) to the Social Health Authority under the Social Health Insurance Act 2023, the new agency inherited critical systemic failures:

Broken member registration - fragmented records across multiple legacy systems
Slow claims processing - 60–90 day payment cycles crippling healthcare providers
Massive fraud losses - estimated at KES 8–10 billion per year
No spatial intelligence - no way to identify coverage gaps or optimize facility distribution across 47 counties

SHA needed a system that could handle three distinct insurance funds (SHIF, PHC, ECCIF), process claims in real time, and detect fraud before payments are made.

title: SHA Integrated Health System
text:
Architecture
I designed a 4-package architecture with clean separation of concerns:
1. shaSystem - R Business Logic Package

Pure R package - the only component that touches the database
Member registration with state machine lifecycle management
6-gate claims adjudication engine: schema validation, member eligibility, facility verification, benefit package lookup (ICD-10 code + facility level + fund type), amount ceiling enforcement, and duplicate detection
Isolation Forest ML fraud detection with feedback loop - confirmed fraud upweighted 3x, false positives downweighted 0.5x
Spatial intelligence via sf/PostGIS: nearest facility search, Voronoi catchment computation, county coverage scoring

2. shaAPI - Plumber REST API

28 JWT-authenticated endpoints covering auth, members, claims, anomaly detection, spatial queries, and USSD access
Zero business logic - every route delegates to shaSystem
Idempotency keys, rate limiting, httpOnly cookie authentication
USSD endpoints for feature phone access (critical for rural Kenya)

3. shaApp - Golem/Shiny Dashboard

9 role-gated modules: overview KPIs, registration, claims queue, payments, anomaly case management, analytics (4 leaflet maps + plotly charts), admin, interoperability, and ops
bslib v5 with SHA brand theming
8 RBAC roles with granular permissions

4. shaMobile - React 18 TypeScript PWA

Tailwind CSS, offline-ready with claim drafts
Bilingual (English/Swahili)
Digital health card with QR code
44px touch targets for mobile-first design

title: SHA Integrated Health System
text:
Key Features

Auto-approval pipeline: Outpatient SHIF/PHC claims under KES 5,000 with anomaly score below 0.65 are automatically approved - reducing processing time from weeks to seconds.


ML Fraud Detection: Isolation Forest model scoring claim amount z-scores, facility volume ratios (Poisson-expected), settlement ratios, diagnosis rarity, and member frequency. Batch scoring processes up to 1,000 claims per run.
Spatial Intelligence: PostGIS-backed nearest facility search, population-within-distance analysis, Voronoi catchments for 47 counties using rKenyaCensus polygons.
Kenya-specific design: M-Pesa payment integration, KMHFR facility registry seeding, ICD-10/ICD-11 mapping weighted to Kenya’s disease burden, compliance with Kenya’s Data Protection Act 2019.
Argon2id password hashing via sodium package for secure authentication.

title: SHA Integrated Health System
text:
Tech Stack

  R
  Plumber
  Golem/Shiny
  isotree
  sf / PostGIS
  leaflet
  React 18
  TypeScript
  Tailwind CSS
  PostgreSQL
  JWT / RBAC
  Docker Compose
  nginx
  M-Pesa API

title: Understanding Classification and Clustering: Decision Trees, Random Forests, SVM, Naive Bayes, and K-Means with Iris Data
text:
Decision Trees
Concept: A decision tree splits the data based on feature values, creating branches and leaves to classify new observations.


Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 1: Define and Fit a Decision Tree Model
# ---------------------------------------------
tree_fit <- decision_tree() %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>%
  set_engine("rpart") %>%
  fit(Species ~ ., data = iris_train)

# --------------------------------------------------
# STEP 2: Visualize the Tree Structure
# --------------------------------------------------
tree_fit$fit %>%
  rpart.plot(extra = 104, roundint = FALSE)









Code
# ------------------------------------------------
# STEP 3: Visualize Feature Importance
# ------------------------------------------------
tree_fit$fit$variable.importance %>%
  enframe(name = "feature", value = "importance") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_reorder(feature, importance), y = importance)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#377eb8") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Feature Importance in Decision Tree",
    x = "Feature",
    y = "Importance Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Code
# --------------------------------------------
# STEP 4: Predict on Test Set
# --------------------------------------------
tree_predictions <- predict(tree_fit, new_data = iris_test) %>%
  bind_cols(iris_test)

# --------------------------------------------
# STEP 5: Evaluate Model Performance
# --------------------------------------------
tree_metrics <- tree_predictions %>%
  metrics(truth = Species, estimate = .pred_class)

# Show metrics table
tree_metrics %>%
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Decision Tree Classification Performance on Test Data"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

  summary() %>%
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Confusion Matrix: Class-Level Metrics"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("condensed", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

title: Understanding Classification and Clustering: Decision Trees, Random Forests, SVM, Naive Bayes, and K-Means with Iris Data
text:
Random Forest
Concept: A random forest is an ensemble of decision trees that improves accuracy by averaging many de-correlated trees.


Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 1: Define and Fit a Random Forest Model
# ---------------------------------------------
rf_fit <- rand_forest(trees = 500, mtry = 2, min_n = 5) %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>%
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity") %>%
  fit(Species ~ ., data = iris_train)

# --------------------------------------------
# STEP 2: Visualize Feature Importance
# --------------------------------------------
rf_fit %>%
  extract_fit_engine() %>%
  vip(num_features = 4, bar = TRUE, aesthetics = list(fill = "#4daf4a")) +
  labs(
    title = "Random Forest Feature Importance (Impurity-Based)",
    x = "Importance",
    y = "Feature"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Code
# --------------------------------------------
# STEP 3: Predict on Test Set
# --------------------------------------------
rf_predictions <- predict(rf_fit, new_data = iris_test) %>%
  bind_cols(iris_test)

# --------------------------------------------
# STEP 4: Evaluate Model Performance
# --------------------------------------------
rf_metrics <- rf_predictions %>%
  metrics(truth = Species, estimate = .pred_class)

# Display performance metrics
rf_metrics %>%
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "Random Forest Classification Performance on Test Data"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

title: Understanding Classification and Clustering: Decision Trees, Random Forests, SVM, Naive Bayes, and K-Means with Iris Data
text:
Support Vector Machine (SVM)
Concept: SVM finds an optimal hyperplane that separates classes with the largest margin in the feature space.


Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 1: Define and Fit an SVM Model (Radial Kernel)
# ---------------------------------------------
svm_fit <- svm_rbf(cost = 1, rbf_sigma = 0.1) %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>%
  set_engine("kernlab") %>%
  fit(Species ~ ., data = iris_train)

# --------------------------------------------
# STEP 2: Predict on Test Set
# --------------------------------------------
svm_predictions <- predict(svm_fit, new_data = iris_test) %>%
  bind_cols(iris_test)

# --------------------------------------------
# STEP 3: Evaluate Model Performance
# --------------------------------------------
svm_metrics <- svm_predictions %>%
  metrics(truth = Species, estimate = .pred_class)

# Show evaluation metrics in table
svm_metrics %>%
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "SVM Classification Performance on Test Data"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

title: Understanding Classification and Clustering: Decision Trees, Random Forests, SVM, Naive Bayes, and K-Means with Iris Data
text:
Naive Bayes
Concept: Naive Bayes is a probabilistic model assuming independence between features given the class label.


Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 1: Define and Fit a Naive Bayes Model
# ---------------------------------------------
nb_fit <- naive_Bayes() %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>%
  set_engine("naivebayes") %>%
  fit(Species ~ ., data = iris_train)

# --------------------------------------------
# STEP 2: Predict on Test Set
# --------------------------------------------
nb_predictions <- predict(nb_fit, new_data = iris_test) %>%
  bind_cols(iris_test)

# --------------------------------------------
# STEP 3: Evaluate Model Performance

  metrics(truth = Species, estimate = .pred_class)

# Display evaluation metrics

title: Understanding Classification and Clustering: Decision Trees, Random Forests, SVM, Naive Bayes, and K-Means with Iris Data
text:
KNN
Concept: K-Nearest Neighbors classifies each observation by majority vote among its k closest training examples.


Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 1: Define and Fit a KNN Model
# ---------------------------------------------
knn_fit <- nearest_neighbor(neighbors = 5, weight_func = "rectangular") %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>%
  set_engine("kknn") %>%
  fit(Species ~ ., data = iris_train)

# --------------------------------------------
# STEP 2: Predict on Test Set
# --------------------------------------------
knn_predictions <- predict(knn_fit, new_data = iris_test) %>%
  bind_cols(iris_test)

# --------------------------------------------
# STEP 3: Evaluate Model Performance
# --------------------------------------------
knn_metrics <- knn_predictions %>%
  metrics(truth = Species, estimate = .pred_class)

# Display performance metrics
knn_metrics %>%
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 3,
    caption = "K-Nearest Neighbors Classification Performance on Test Data"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

title: Understanding Classification and Clustering: Decision Trees, Random Forests, SVM, Naive Bayes, and K-Means with Iris Data
text:
K-Means Clustering
Concept: K-means is an unsupervised method that groups observations into k clusters based on similarity.


Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 1: Scale Data (Exclude labels)
# ---------------------------------------------
iris_scaled <- iris %>%
  select(-Species) %>%
  scale()

# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 2: Apply K-Means Clustering (k = 3)
# ---------------------------------------------
set.seed(123)
kmeans_fit <- kmeans(iris_scaled, centers = 3, nstart = 25)

# Add cluster assignments to original data
iris_clustered <- iris %>%
  mutate(
    cluster = as.factor(kmeans_fit$cluster),
    pca1 = prcomp(iris_scaled)$x[, 1],
    pca2 = prcomp(iris_scaled)$x[, 2]
  )

# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 3: Visualize K-Means Clusters via PCA
# ---------------------------------------------
fviz_cluster(
  kmeans_fit,
  data = iris_scaled,
  geom = "point",
  ellipse.type = "norm",
  show.clust.cent = TRUE,
  palette = "jco",
  ggtheme = theme_minimal()
) +
  labs(
    title = "K-Means Clustering of Iris Data",
    subtitle = "Clusters visualized with PCA"
  )









Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 4: Compare Clusters to True Species
# ---------------------------------------------
table(True_Label = iris$Species, Cluster = iris_clustered$cluster) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Cluster, values_from = Freq) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Cluster Composition by True Iris Species",
    align = "lccc"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)



Cluster Composition by True Iris Species

Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 5: Visualize True Species in PCA Space
# ---------------------------------------------
ggplot(iris_clustered, aes(x = pca1, y = pca2, color = Species)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "True Iris Species Visualized in PCA Space",
    x = "Principal Component 1",
    y = "Principal Component 2"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 6: Visualize K-Means Clusters in PCA Space
# ---------------------------------------------
ggplot(iris_clustered, aes(x = pca1, y = pca2, color = cluster)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "K-Means Cluster Assignments in PCA Space",
    x = "Principal Component 1",
    y = "Principal Component 2"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 7: Silhouette Score Evaluation
# ---------------------------------------------
silhouette_score <- silhouette(kmeans_fit$cluster, dist(iris_scaled))

fviz_silhouette(silhouette_score) +
  labs(title = "Silhouette Plot for K-Means Clustering")


  cluster size ave.sil.width
1       1   50          0.64
2       2   53          0.39
3       3   47          0.35

title: Understanding Classification and Clustering: Decision Trees, Random Forests, SVM, Naive Bayes, and K-Means with Iris Data
text:
Hierarchical Clustering
Concept: Hierarchical clustering builds a nested tree of clusters (dendrogram) by iteratively merging the most similar groups.


Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 1: Prepare and Scale Data
# ---------------------------------------------
iris_scaled <- iris %>%
  select(-Species) %>%
  scale()

# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 2: Compute Distance Matrix
# ---------------------------------------------
iris_dist <- dist(iris_scaled, method = "euclidean")

# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 3: Apply Hierarchical Clustering (Complete Linkage)
# ---------------------------------------------
hc_fit <- hclust(iris_dist, method = "complete")

# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 4: Plot the Dendrogram
# ---------------------------------------------
fviz_dend(
  hc_fit,
  k = 3,                            # cut tree into 3 clusters
  cex = 0.7,                        # label size
  k_colors = c("#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "#FC4E07"),
  color_labels_by_k = TRUE,
  rect = TRUE,
  rect_border = "gray30",
  rect_fill = TRUE
) +
  labs(title = "Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram (Complete Linkage)")









Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 5: Assign Clusters and Add to Original Data
# ---------------------------------------------
iris_clustered <- iris %>%
  mutate(
    cluster = as.factor(cutree(hc_fit, k = 3)),
    pca1 = prcomp(iris_scaled)$x[, 1],
    pca2 = prcomp(iris_scaled)$x[, 2]
  )

# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 6: Visualize Cluster Assignments in PCA Space
# ---------------------------------------------
ggplot(iris_clustered, aes(x = pca1, y = pca2, color = cluster)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Hierarchical Cluster Assignments in PCA Space",
    x = "Principal Component 1",
    y = "Principal Component 2"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Code
# ---------------------------------------------
# STEP 7: Compare Clusters to True Species
# ---------------------------------------------
table(True_Label = iris$Species, Cluster = iris_clustered$cluster) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Cluster, values_from = Freq) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Cluster Composition by True Iris Species (Hierarchical Clustering)",
    align = "lccc"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)



Cluster Composition by True Iris Species (Hierarchical Clustering)


True_Label

title: Understanding Classification and Clustering: Decision Trees, Random Forests, SVM, Naive Bayes, and K-Means with Iris Data
text:
Conclusion
This exploration showcases how various machine learning models differ in their assumptions, complexity, and ideal use cases. There’s no single “best” model — the right choice depends on your goals, data, and constraints.
Here’s a practical guide:

Use Decision Trees when interpretability and clear rules are a priority.
Use Random Forests for higher accuracy, especially when variance is a concern.
Use Support Vector Machines (SVMs) when decision boundaries are complex or nonlinear.
Use Naive Bayes when you need a fast, simple, and often surprisingly effective baseline.
Use K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) for intuitive distance-based predictions — but be cautious with scaling and noisy features.
Use K-Means or Hierarchical Clustering when your data is unlabeled, but you suspect structure or groupings. K-Means is fast and intuitive, but assumes spherical clusters while Hierarchical Clustering gives a dendrogram you can interpret visually, useful for small to medium datasets.

title: Understanding Classification and Clustering: Decision Trees, Random Forests, SVM, Naive Bayes, and K-Means with Iris Data
text:
Summary Table


Code
model_performance <- bind_rows(
  tree_metrics %>% mutate(model = "Decision Tree"),
  rf_metrics %>% mutate(model = "Random Forest"),
  svm_metrics %>% mutate(model = "SVM"),
  nb_metrics %>% mutate(model = "Naive Bayes"),
  knn_metrics %>% mutate(model = "KNN")
)

model_performance %>%
  filter(.metric == "accuracy") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = model, y = .estimate, fill = model)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Model Accuracy Comparison", x = NULL, y = "Accuracy") +
  theme_minimal()










The table below summarizes the key differences in modeling approaches explored in this post:





Comparison of Classification and Clustering Models


Model
Type
Key Idea
Strengths
Weaknesses




Decision Tree
Supervised
Rule-based split on features
Interpretable, fast
Can overfit


Random Forest
Supervised
Ensemble of decision trees
High accuracy, handles variance
Less interpretable


SVM
Supervised
Optimal separating hyperplane
Great for complex boundaries
Sensitive to tuning


Naive Bayes
Supervised
Probabilistic with independence assumption
Simple, interpretable
Assumes independence


KNN
Supervised
Majority vote among k-nearest neighbors
Simple, no training needed
Sensitive to scale and noise


K-Means
Unsupervised
Centroid-based clustering
Fast, intuitive
Needs predefined k, sensitive to scaling


Hierarchical Clustering
Unsupervised
Nested tree of similarities
No need to pre-specify k, interpretable dendrogram
Computationally expensive for large datasets

title: SmartLicenSync - Revenue Optimizer
text:
Full Stack Developer • Systems Designer
  
  A comprehensive business permit and license management platform built for the Kericho County Government - digitizing own-source revenue collection across 30 wards and 6 sub-counties.
  🔒 Private Repository

title: SmartLicenSync - Revenue Optimizer
text:
Problem
County governments in Kenya face persistent challenges with own-source revenue:

Manual permit processing - paper-based systems prone to errors, fraud, and revenue leakage
No compliance tracking - no way to monitor which businesses have valid permits or are overdue
No spatial visibility - county officials cannot map business distribution or identify underserved wards
Fragmented payments - M-Pesa, bank transfers, and cash payments tracked in separate systems
No audit trail - impossible to trace who approved what, when, and why

Kericho County needed a unified digital platform to replace manual workflows and optimize revenue collection.

title: SmartLicenSync - Revenue Optimizer
text:
Solution
I designed and built a deployment-ready Golem/Shiny application with 11 integrated modules:
Core Modules:

Executive Dashboard - KPI boxes for total revenue, active businesses, compliance rate, growth rate
Business Registration - Full portal with Kenya-specific validations (KRA PIN, phone format, Kericho County GPS bounds)
Payment Integration - M-Pesa, bank transfers, cash, cheque, and online payments with receipt validation
Compliance Engine - Monitors compliant, overdue, expiring, and violated businesses by ward and status
Business Mapping - GIS/geospatial view of business locations via PostGIS + Leaflet
Reports & Analytics - Revenue YTD, monthly averages, growth rate, compliance rate

Platform Architecture:

Golem framework - production-grade Shiny app structure with modular architecture
bs4Dash - Bootstrap 4 admin dashboard UI with sidebar navigation
PostgreSQL 15 with PostGIS - spatially-enabled database, containerized via Docker Compose
Connection pooling via DBI + pool, with migration system, health checks, and backup utilities
Full audit logging - INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE with before/after JSONB snapshots, IP, and user agent tracking
Role-based access control - system, administrative, financial, analytical, and data entry roles

title: SmartLicenSync - Revenue Optimizer
text:
Domain-Specific Design

Built for Kericho County: 30 wards across 6 sub-counties (Kipkelion East/West, Ainamoi, Bureti, Sigowet/Soin, Kericho), with 24 Kenya-specific business categories including duka/kiosk, matatu operations, boda boda, M-Pesa agent, SACCO, tea processing, and dairy/milk collection.


Auto-generated registration numbers: KER{YEAR}{SEQUENCE}
Fee structures from KES 1,000 (small duka) to KES 20,000 (large manufacturing)
M-Pesa receipt validation and callback integration
KRA PIN regex validation (^[A-Z][0-9]{9}[A-Z]$)
Kenyan phone number format (^254[0-9]{9}$)
GPS coordinate bounds locked to Kericho County
